statement.markdown: Given a binary tree, return the leftmost and rightmost nodes at each level. The output should be two lists, one for the left view and one for the right view.
statement.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer representing the number of nodes in the binary tree. The next lines contain the node values in a level-order traversal (BFS) format, with 'X' representing null nodes.
statement.outputFormat: Two lists of node values, one for the left view and one for the right view, separated by a newline character. Each list should be on a separate line, with node values separated by spaces.
statement.notes: The binary tree node values are integers, and 'X' represents null nodes in the input.

constraints.description: The number of nodes in the binary tree.
The values of the nodes in the binary tree.

examples.explanation: The binary tree has the following structure:       1     /   \    2     3   / \   4   5 The left view is [1, 2, 4] and the right view is [1, 3, 5].
The binary tree has the following structure:       1     /   \    2     3 The left view is [1, 2] and the right view is [1, 3].

hints.text: To solve this problem, you should first understand the structure of a binary tree and how to traverse it level by level.
A suitable traversal method for this problem is Breadth-First Search (BFS), as it naturally processes nodes level by level. You can utilize a queue data structure to implement BFS.

During the BFS traversal, for each level, you need to keep track of the first and last node you encounter. The first node will contribute to the left view, and the last node will contribute to the right view.

The core technique here involves using a data structure (like a queue) to perform level-order traversal and keeping track of the first and last nodes at each level. Consider how you can modify the BFS algorithm to specifically capture these nodes.

editorial.title: Right/Left View of Binary Tree
editorial.intuition: The problem requires finding the leftmost and rightmost nodes at each level of a binary tree. A brute force approach would involve traversing the tree level by level and storing the first and last node at each level.
editorial.bruteForce: The brute force approach would involve using a level order traversal (BFS) to visit each node in the tree. At each level, we would store the first and last node's value. This approach is correct but slow because it still requires visiting every node in the tree, resulting in a time complexity of O(n) but with a higher constant factor due to the overhead of storing and retrieving the first and last node at each level.
editorial.betterSolution: The key observation that unlocks the optimal approach is that we only need to keep track of the first and last node at each level. We can do this by maintaining a queue of nodes to visit and storing the first and last node's value at each level.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution uses a level order traversal (BFS) approach with a queue to visit each node in the tree. By keeping track of the first and last node at each level, we can efficiently compute the left and right views of the binary tree in O(n) time and O(n) space.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the optimal solution can be proven by induction on the levels of the tree. At each level, we correctly identify the first and last node by visiting all nodes at that level. The base case is the root node, which is trivially correct. The inductive step follows by assuming correctness for all levels up to k and showing correctness for level k+1, which follows from the fact that we visit all nodes at each level.
editorial.dryRun: A dry run of the optimal solution on a sample binary tree confirms that it produces the correct left and right views. For example, on a tree with levels [[1], [2, 3], [4, 5, 6, 7]], the solution correctly identifies the left view as [1, 2, 4] and the right view as [1, 3, 7].
commonMistakes: Not handling the case where the tree is empty
Not correctly identifying the first and last node at each level
Using a recursive approach instead of an iterative one, which can lead to stack overflow for large trees
Not using a queue to efficiently visit nodes at each level
Not storing the first and last node's value at each level
Not handling the case where a node has only one child

functionSignature.cpp: vector<int> leftView(TreeNode* root); vector<int> rightView(TreeNode* root);
functionSignature.java: public List<Integer> leftView(TreeNode root); public List<Integer> rightView(TreeNode root);
functionSignature.python: def leftView(self, root: TreeNode) -> List[int]: def rightView(self, root: TreeNode) -> List[int]:
functionSignature.javascript: var leftView = function(root); var rightView = function(root);
